title: Video game genre

infobox.value: is an informal classification of a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") based on how it is played rather than [visual](/wiki/Computer_graphics "Computer graphics") or [narrative](/wiki/Narrative "Narrative") elements.[[1]](#cite_note-Apperley-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Gamasutra-2) This is independent of [setting](/wiki/Setting_(fiction) "Setting (fiction)"), unlike works of [fiction](/wiki/Fiction "Fiction") that are expressed through other media, such as [films](/wiki/Film "Film") or [books](/wiki/Book "Book"). For example, a [shooter game](/wiki/Shooter_game "Shooter game") is still a shooter game, regardless of where or when it takes place.[[3]](#cite_note-fundamentals-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) A specific game's genre is open to subjective interpretation. An individual game may belong to several genres at once.[[1]](#cite_note-Apperley-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

*[StepMania](/wiki/StepMania "StepMania")*, a [rhythm game](/wiki/Rhythm_game "Rhythm game")

A **video game genre** is an informal classification of a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") based on how it is played rather than [visual](/wiki/Computer_graphics "Computer graphics") or [narrative](/wiki/Narrative "Narrative") elements.[[1]](#cite_note-Apperley-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Gamasutra-2) This is independent of [setting](/wiki/Setting_(fiction) "Setting (fiction)"), unlike works of [fiction](/wiki/Fiction "Fiction") that are expressed through other media, such as [films](/wiki/Film "Film") or [books](/wiki/Book "Book"). For example, a [shooter game](/wiki/Shooter_game "Shooter game") is still a shooter game, regardless of where or when it takes place.[[3]](#cite_note-fundamentals-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) A specific game's genre is open to subjective interpretation. An individual game may belong to several genres at once.[[1]](#cite_note-Apperley-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_genre&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Early attempts at categorizing video games were primarily for organizing catalogs and books. A 1981 catalog for the [Atari Video Computer System](/wiki/Atari_2600 "Atari 2600") uses 8 headings: Skill Gallery, Space Station, Classics Corner, Adventure Territory, Race Track, Sports Arena, Combat Zone, and Learning Center.[[5]](#cite_note-5) ("Classics", in this case, refers to chess and checkers.) In Tom Hirschfeld's 1981 book *[How to Master the Video Games](/wiki/How_to_Master_the_Video_Games "How to Master the Video Games")*, he divides the games into broad categories in the table of contents: *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")*-type, *[Asteroids](/wiki/Asteroids_(video_game) "Asteroids (video game)")*-type, maze, reflex, and miscellaneous.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The first two of these correspond to the still-used genres of [fixed shooter](/wiki/Fixed_shooter "Fixed shooter") and [multidirectional shooter](/wiki/Multidirectional_shooter "Multidirectional shooter").

For home computers, two publications established a small number of categories based on the best-selling software in the early 1980s: *[Softalk](/wiki/Softalk "Softalk")*, which ran its Top Thirty list from 1980 to 1984 with the genres of strategy, adventure, fantasy and arcade; and *[Computer Gaming World](/wiki/Computer_Gaming_World "Computer Gaming World")*, which collected user-submitted rankings. *Computer Gaming World* initially used three categories in 1981—arcade, wargame, and adventure—but by 1989 had expanded its genre list to strategy, simulation, adventure, role-playing adventure, wargame, and action/arcade.

[Chris Crawford](/wiki/Chris_Crawford_(game_designer) "Chris Crawford (game designer)") attempted to classify video games in his 1984 book *[The Art of Computer Game Design](/wiki/The_Art_of_Computer_Game_Design "The Art of Computer Game Design")*. Crawford focused on the player's experience and activities required for gameplay.[[7]](#cite_note-Explosion-7) He wrote, "the state of computer game design is changing quickly. We would therefore expect the taxonomy presented [in this book] to become obsolete or inadequate in a short time."[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo"), in bringing its [Family Computer](/wiki/Family_Computer "Family Computer") system into the North American market as the [Nintendo Entertainment System](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System") in 1985, looked to avoid the issues with loss of publishing control that had led to the [video game crash of 1983](/wiki/Video_game_crash_of_1983 "Video game crash of 1983") and to prevent unauthorized games for the system.[[9]](#cite_note-9) To solve this, Nintendo required approval of all games for the NES.[[2]](#cite_note-Gamasutra-2) To support this, Nintendo classified games into eight major series: Adventure, Action, Sports, Light-Gun, Programmable, Arcade, Robot, and Educational. The series description appeared on early "black box" covers and subsequently in the *NES Player's Guide*.[[10]](#cite_note-10) By the time of the [Game Boy](/wiki/Game_Boy "Game Boy") and [Super NES](/wiki/Super_NES "Super NES"), Nintendo had retired the Arcade, Light-Gun, Robot, Programmable, and Educational series, but added RPG & Simulation and Puzzle.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Consoles manufacturers that followed the NES followed similar behavior in requiring licenses to develop games for their systems. To assure they would get these licenses, console developers tended to stay with gameplay of previously published games for that console, thus causing groups of games within the same genre to grow.[[2]](#cite_note-Gamasutra-2)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Subsequently, retailers displayed games grouped by genres, and market research firms found that players had preferences for certain types over others, based on region, and developers could plan out future strategies through this.[[2]](#cite_note-Gamasutra-2)

With the industry expanding in the 1990s and budgets for video games began growing, large [publishers](/wiki/Video_game_publisher "Video game publisher") like [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts") began to form to handle the marketing and publication of games, both for consoles and personal computers. Targeting high-value, low-risk video game genres was key for some publishers, and small and independent developers were typically forced to compete by abandoning more experimental gameplay and settling into the same genres used by larger publishers.[[2]](#cite_note-Gamasutra-2)

As hardware capabilities have increased, new genres have become possible, with examples being increased [memory](/wiki/Computer_memory "Computer memory"), the move from 2D to 3D, new [peripherals](/wiki/Peripheral "Peripheral"), [online](/wiki/Online_game "Online game") functionalities, and [location-based](/wiki/Location-based_game "Location-based game") mechanics.[[2]](#cite_note-Gamasutra-2) Experimental gameplay from [indie game development](/wiki/Indie_game_development "Indie game development") drew more attention in the late 2000s and 2010s aided by independent digital distribution, as large publishers focused on [triple-A](/wiki/AAA_(video_game_industry) "AAA (video game industry)") titles were extremely risk-averse.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Through indie games, a revival of experimental gameplay had emerged, and several new genres have emerged since then.[[2]](#cite_note-Gamasutra-2)

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_genre&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Definition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Doom_clone_vs_first_person_shooter.png/250px-Doom_clone_vs_first_person_shooter.png)](/wiki/File:Doom_clone_vs_first_person_shooter.png)

The use of "*Doom* clone" (red) versus "first-person shooter" (blue) over time

Due to "direct and active participation" of the player, video game genres differ from [literary](/wiki/Literary_genre "Literary genre") and [film genres](/wiki/Film_genre "Film genre").[[7]](#cite_note-Explosion-7) Though one could state that *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")* is a [science fiction video game](/wiki/Science_fiction_video_game "Science fiction video game"), author Mark J.P. Wolf wrote that such a classification "ignores the fundamental differences and similarities which are to be found in the player's experience of the game".[[7]](#cite_note-Explosion-7) In contrast to the visual aesthetics of games, which can vary greatly, it is argued that it is interactivity characteristics that are common to all games.[[1]](#cite_note-Apperley-1)

Like film genres, the names of video game genres have come about generally as a common understanding between the audience and the producers.[[7]](#cite_note-Explosion-7) Descriptive names of genres take into account the goals of the game, the protagonist and even the perspective offered to the player. For example, a [first-person shooter](/wiki/First-person_shooter "First-person shooter") is a game that is played from a [first-person perspective](/wiki/First-person_perspective "First-person perspective") and involves the practice of shooting.[[15]](#cite_note-Revealed-15) Whereas "[shooter game](/wiki/Shooter_game "Shooter game")" is a genre name, "first-person shooter" and "[third-person shooter](/wiki/Third-person_shooter "Third-person shooter")" are common subgenres of the shooter genre.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Other examples of such prefixes are [real-time](/wiki/Real-time_game "Real-time game"), [turn based](/wiki/Turn-based_game "Turn-based game"), [top-down](/wiki/Top-down_perspective "Top-down perspective") and [side-scrolling](/wiki/Side-scrolling_game "Side-scrolling game").

Genre names may evolve over time. The [platform game](/wiki/Platform_game "Platform game") genre started as "climbing games", based on Steve Bloom's 1982 book *Video Invaders*, as they were inspired by games like *[Donkey Kong](/wiki/Donkey_Kong_(arcade_game) "Donkey Kong (arcade game)")* with ladders and jumping.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The same term was used by the US and UK press in 1983, including magazines *[Electronic Games](/wiki/Electronic_Games "Electronic Games")* and *TV Gamer*.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) First-person shooters were originally known as "*Doom* clones" in the years following 1993's *[Doom](/wiki/Doom_(1993_video_game) "Doom (1993 video game)")*, while the term "first-person shooters" became more common by around 2000.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

New genres emerge continuously throughout the history of video games, often due to the cross-pollination of ideas borrowed from different games into new ones. For example, the seminal [text-based](/wiki/Text-based_game "Text-based game") [adventure game](/wiki/Adventure_game "Adventure game") *[Colossal Cave Adventure](/wiki/Colossal_Cave_Adventure "Colossal Cave Adventure")* directly inspired the [Atari VCS](/wiki/Atari_VCS "Atari VCS") game *[Adventure](/wiki/Adventure_(1980_video_game) "Adventure (1980 video game)")*, but incorporating joystick control as in an [action game](/wiki/Action_game "Action game") rather than typed commands. *Adventure* served as the prototype of the [action-adventure game](/wiki/Action-adventure_game "Action-adventure game") genre that would be popularized by *[The Legend of Zelda](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda_(video_game) "The Legend of Zelda (video game)")*.[[22]](#cite_note-routledge_chp29-22)

The target audience, underlying theme or purpose of a game are sometimes used as a genre identifier, such as with "[Christian game](/wiki/Christian_video_game "Christian video game")" and "[serious game](/wiki/Serious_game "Serious game")" respectively. However, because these terms do not indicate anything about the gameplay of a video game, these are not considered genres.[[2]](#cite_note-Gamasutra-2)

## Classifications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_genre&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Classifications")]

Main article: [List of video game genres](/wiki/List_of_video_game_genres "List of video game genres")

Video game genres vary in specificity, with popular [video game reviews](/wiki/Video_game_journalism "Video game journalism") using genre names varying from "[action](/wiki/Action_game "Action game")" to "baseball". In this practice, basic themes and more fundamental characteristics are used alongside each other.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

A game may combine aspects of multiple genres in such a way that it becomes hard to classify under existing genres. For example, because *[Grand Theft Auto III](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto_III "Grand Theft Auto III")* combined shooting, driving and roleplaying in an unusual way, it was hard to classify using existing terms. The term [*Grand Theft Auto* clone](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto_clone "Grand Theft Auto clone") has been used to describe games mechanically similar to *Grand Theft Auto III*.[[15]](#cite_note-Revealed-15) Similarly, the term [roguelike](/wiki/Roguelike "Roguelike") has been developed for games that share similarities with [*Rogue*](/wiki/Rogue_(game) "Rogue (game)").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Elements of the [role-playing](/wiki/Role-playing_video_game "Role-playing video game") genre, which focuses on storytelling and character growth, have been implemented in many different genres of video games. This is because the addition of a story and character enhancement to an action, [strategy](/wiki/Strategy_video_game "Strategy video game") or [puzzle video game](/wiki/Puzzle_video_game "Puzzle video game") does not take away from its core gameplay, but adds an incentive other than survival to the experience.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In addition to gameplay elements, some games may be categorized by other schemes; such are typically not used as genres:[[1]](#cite_note-Apperley-1)

* By platform: generally to the nature of the computer hardware that the game is played on and not the specific branding. This would include game genres like [mobile games](/wiki/Mobile_game "Mobile game") for [smartphone](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone"), [tablet computers](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"), or other similar portable devices; and [browser games](/wiki/Browser_game "Browser game") that can be played in a [web browser](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser"). Identification of the type of hardware a game is played on implied certain limits to the type of gameplay that is available; a mobile game will typically lack as much action compared to a game playable on a home console or computer due to limitations on player input.
* By mode: referring to whether a game is [single player](/wiki/Single-player_video_game "Single-player video game"), [multiplayer](/wiki/Multiplayer_video_game "Multiplayer video game"), or variations on that, including [massively multiplayer online](/wiki/Massively_multiplayer_online "Massively multiplayer online") (MMO) games, [cooperative games](/wiki/Cooperative_video_game "Cooperative video game"), [player versus environment](/wiki/Player_versus_environment "Player versus environment") (PvE) or [player versus player](/wiki/Player_versus_player "Player versus player") (PvP) games, and so forth.
* By narrative: Classifying video games by their narrative style, such as science fiction or fantasy, is typically not used within the field, with the key exception of [horror games](/wiki/Horror_game "Horror game"), which broadly cover any game dealing with elements of [horror fiction](/wiki/Horror_fiction "Horror fiction").

## Popularity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_genre&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Popularity")]

According to some analysts, the percentage of each broad genre in the best-selling physical games worldwide is broken down as follows.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

| Genre | Softalk 1980-1984 | VGC top 100 | | | | | | | | ESA | Sta |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1995 | 2000 | 2005 | 2010 | 2015 | 2016 | 2017 | 2018 |
| [Action](/wiki/Action_game "Action game") | 61 | 3 | 4 | 12 | 15 | 27 | 25 | 22 | 29 | 22.5 | 26.9 |
| [Adventure](/wiki/Adventure_game "Adventure game") | 11 | 2 | 4 | 7 | 6 | 2 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 7.8 | 7.9 |
| [Fighting](/wiki/Fighting_game "Fighting game") |  | 15 | 10 | 5 | 2 | 5 | 3 | 5 | 5 | 5.8 | 7.8 |
| [Platform](/wiki/Platform_game "Platform game") |  | 10 | 7 | 10 | 9 | 4 | 3 | 4 | 9 |  |  |
| [Puzzle](/wiki/Puzzle_video_game "Puzzle video game") |  | 9 | 2 | 6 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |  |  |
| [Racing](/wiki/Racing_video_game "Racing video game") |  | 6 | 6 | 13 | 8 | 5 | 4 | 6 | 6 | 3.3 | 5.8 |
| [Role-playing](/wiki/Role-playing_video_game "Role-playing video game") | 18 | 18 | 25 | 7 | 16 | 12 | 15 | 17 | 12 | 12.9 | 11.3 |
| [Shooter](/wiki/Shooter_game "Shooter game") |  | 11 | 1 | 8 | 14 | 22 | 24 | 19 | 13 | 27.5 | 20.9 |
| [Simulation](/wiki/Simulation_video_game "Simulation video game") |  | 6 | 7 | 5 | 0 | 4 | 4 | 0 | 2 |  |  |
| [Sports](/wiki/Sports_game "Sports game") |  | 9 | 19 | 17 | 16 | 12 | 13 | 15 | 13 | 11.7 | 11.1 |
| [Strategy](/wiki/Strategy_video_game "Strategy video game") | 10 | 7 | 8 | 3 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 2 | 1 | 4.3 | 3.7 |
| Misc |  | 4 | 7 | 7 | 12 | 7 | 8 | 9 | 8 | 4.1 | 4.6 |

In the last decade, puzzle games have declined when measured by sales, however, on [mobile](/wiki/Mobile_gaming "Mobile gaming"), where the majority of games are [free-to-play](/wiki/Free-to-play "Free-to-play"), this genre remains the most popular worldwide.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)
